text.title: Adapter, Bridge, and Composite Patterns
text.summary: Adapter converts an incompatible interface (retrofit, post-design). Bridge separates abstraction from implementation into independent hierarchies (design-time decision). Composite represents tree structures with uniform leaf/branch treatment.

rule.statement: Use the Adapter pattern to make an incompatible existing interface work with a new interface, without modifying the existing code. Adapter is a retrofit pattern — it is applied after the incompatibility is discovered, not during initial design.
rule.rationale: Third-party libraries and legacy systems have fixed interfaces. Modifying them is impossible or risky. The Adapter wraps them and translates the interface, preserving the existing code's behavior while enabling it to participate in new code contracts.

list.item: Target: the interface the client expects
Adaptee: the existing class with an incompatible interface
Adapter: wraps the Adaptee and implements the Target interface
Object Adapter: wraps the Adaptee instance (composition) — prefer this; more flexible
Class Adapter: inherits from both Target and Adaptee — only in languages with multiple inheritance

example.note: Payment gateway adapter — wraps StripeClient to implement the PaymentProvider target

rule.statement: Use Bridge when two independent dimensions of variation exist and both must be extensible independently. Bridge is a design-time pattern — define it before building the class hierarchy, not as a retrofit to an existing hierarchy.
rule.rationale: Without Bridge, adding a new dimension multiplies the class count: N notification types × M urgency levels = N×M classes. Bridge splits the abstraction (notification type) from the implementation (urgency rendering) into independent hierarchies that can grow independently.

list.item: Abstraction: the high-level control class that delegates to Implementor
Implementor: the interface for implementation classes
Concrete Implementors: the actual implementations (EmailSender, SMSSender)
Refined Abstractions: extended control classes (UrgentNotification, BatchNotification)
Key distinction from Adapter: Bridge is designed up-front; Adapter fixes an existing mismatch

rule.statement: Use Composite when clients must treat individual objects (leaves) and compositions (branches) uniformly. The key constraint: both leaf and branch must implement the same interface so client code needs no type-checking.
rule.rationale: Without Composite, client code must distinguish leaves from branches via isinstance checks, violating OCP. The Composite interface lets clients call the same method on a single file or an entire directory tree — the traversal logic is encapsulated in the composite node.

list.item: Component: the common interface — both Leaf and Composite implement it
Leaf: individual object with no children — implements Component directly
Composite: a node that contains children (Leaf or Composite) — implements Component by delegating to children
Use cases: file systems, UI component trees, organizational hierarchies, expression trees
